schools.summary: Jabin of Hazor gathering the kings of the north against Israel: 'and it came to pass, when Jabin king of Hazor had heard those things, that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Achshaph' — Jabin, hearing of Israel's victories, summoned the northern kings to make war (the and it came to pass, when Jabin king of Hazor had heard those things, Jabin hearing of Israel, the that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, and to the king of Shimron, and to the king of Achshaph, the kings summoned, the figure, the northern kings leagued together against Israel, the great confederacy, the truth that the conquest of the south stirred the north to make war, the kings combining, the figure of the world arrayed against the people of God, the nations gathered, 'the kings of the earth set themselves... against the LORD,' Psalm 2:2, the league of the north, the war prepared, the great confederacy against Israel, vv.4-5); Jabin hearing of Israel's victories (the alarm) — the tidings (those things heard); 'he sent to Jobab king of Madon... and to the king of Achshaph' (the northern kings summoned to war, the league, Psalm 2:2, the figure, the world arrayed against God's people, the providence, the league, the kings of the mountains and plains to follow, vv.2-3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The vast host gathered with horses and chariots: 'and they went out, they and all their hosts with them, much people, even as the sand that is upon the sea shore in multitude, with horses and chariots very many' — a vast army, like the sand of the sea, with many horses and chariots, came out against Israel (the and they went out, they and all their hosts with them, much people, the great host, the even as the sand that is upon the sea shore in multitude, numberless as the sand, the with horses and chariots very many, horses and chariots, the figure, the host numberless as the sand, the horses and chariots a terror, the might of the world, the truth that the confederacy mustered an overwhelming force, the odds against Israel great, the figure of the world's might arrayed against God's people, the horses and chariots the strength of man, 'some trust in chariots... but we will remember the name of the LORD,' Psalm 20:7, the vast army, the great trial of faith, the host against Israel, vv.5); the host numberless as the sand (the multitude, Psalm 20:7) — the multitude (as the sand); 'with horses and chariots very many' (the horses and chariots, the strength of man, the world's might, the figure, the world's might against God's people, the providence, the host, God's word not to fear to follow, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: God's charge not to fear the great host, with the command to hamstring the horses and burn the chariots: 'and the LORD said unto Joshua, Be not afraid because of them: for to morrow about this time will I deliver them up all slain before Israel: thou shalt hough their horses, and burn their chariots with fire' — the renunciation of carnal strength — 'and the LORD said unto Joshua, Be not afraid because of them: for to morrow about this time will I deliver them up all slain before Israel: thou shalt hough their horses, and burn their chariots with fire': God promised the victory and commanded Joshua to destroy the very horses and chariots in which the foe trusted (the witnesses noting the significance — the encouragement of faith against the great host, and the deliberate destruction of the carnal strength, that Israel might trust in God alone, the horses houghed and the chariots burnt, not kept for Israel's use, lest the people trust in them, the lesson — that the people of God are not to trust in the arm of flesh, in horses and chariots, but in the LORD, the very instruments of the world's might destroyed, 'some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD our God,' Psalm 20:7, the truth that God will not have his people lean on carnal power, the horses and chariots forbidden to the king, 'he shall not multiply horses to himself,' Deuteronomy 17:16, the figure — the destruction of the means of self-reliance, that the glory be God's alone, the victory not by might nor by power, 'not by might, nor by power, but by my spirit, saith the LORD,' Zechariah 4:6, the truth that faith renounces the help of the world and rests on God, the chariots burnt, the figure of the believer's renunciation of carnal confidence, the call to trust in God and not in the strength of man, the warning that to keep the world's weapons is to be tempted to trust them, the figure of faith that does not trust in the arm of flesh but in the LORD); God's promise of victory and the charge not to fear the great host, the deliverance assured (the encouragement of faith, Psalm 20:7) — the promise (be not afraid); 'thou shalt hough their horses, and burn their chariots with fire' (the horses houghed and the chariots burnt, the instruments of the world's might destroyed, that Israel trust in God alone and not in carnal power, the renunciation of the arm of flesh, Deuteronomy 17:16; Zechariah 4:6) — the figure (the believer's renunciation of carnal confidence, the providence, the charge, Joshua falling on them suddenly to follow, vv.7) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: Why does God command the horses houghed and the chariots burnt?

sides.position: That Israel trust not in the arm of flesh but in the LORD - the deliberate destruction of the carnal strength in which the foe trusted, the horses and chariots forbidden and destroyed lest God's people lean on carnal power (Psalm 20:7; Deuteronomy 17:16), the victory not by might nor power but by God's Spirit (Zechariah 4:6), a figure of the believer's renunciation of carnal confidence

schools.summary: That Joshua left nothing undone of all the LORD commanded Moses: 'as the LORD commanded Moses his servant, so did Moses command Joshua, and so did Joshua; he left nothing undone of all that the LORD commanded Moses' — the complete obedience — 'as the LORD commanded Moses his servant, so did Moses command Joshua, and so did Joshua; he left nothing undone of all that the LORD commanded Moses': Joshua carried out the whole command of God given through Moses, omitting nothing (the witnesses noting the significance — the entire, exact, and persevering obedience of Joshua, the chain of command from God to Moses to Joshua kept unbroken, nothing left undone, the command given by God to Moses, by Moses to Joshua, and fully performed by Joshua, the whole will of God done, the lesson — that the faithful servant of God does all that God commands, withholding and omitting nothing, the obedience entire and not partial, Joshua's faithfulness a pattern, 'he left nothing undone,' the whole command kept, the truth that true obedience does the whole will of God, not the part that pleases, 'I have finished the work which thou gavest me to do,' John 17:4, the figure — the perfect obedience fulfilled supremely in Christ, who did all the Father's will and left nothing undone, 'I do always those things that please him,' John 8:29, the truth that the servant is to be faithful in all things, the steward who does his lord's whole will, 'well done, thou good and faithful servant,' Matthew 25:21, the figure of the entire obedience that omits nothing of God's command, the call to do the whole will of God, withholding nothing, the warning against the partial obedience that does some and leaves the rest, the figure of the entire and faithful obedience of the servant of God); the command passing from God to Moses to Joshua, the line of authority unbroken, the whole will of God transmitted (the chain of command, John 17:4) — the command (from God through Moses); 'and so did Joshua; he left nothing undone of all that the LORD commanded Moses' (Joshua doing all, leaving nothing undone, the entire and faithful obedience of the servant, fulfilled supremely in Christ who finished the Father's work, the perfect obedience, John 8:29; Matthew 25:21) — the figure (the obedience fulfilled in Christ who left nothing undone, the providence, the obedience, the summary of the conquest and the kings to follow, vv.16-23) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'he left nothing undone' establish about Joshua?

sides.position: The entire and faithful obedience of the servant of God - Joshua carried out the whole command of God given through Moses, omitting nothing, the chain of command from God to Moses to Joshua kept unbroken, true obedience that does all God commands (John 17:4), a figure of the perfect obedience fulfilled supremely in Christ who did all the Father's will and finished the work (John 8:29; Matthew 25:21)

the conquest not won in a day but a long war with the kings
the patient warfare of God's people against the powers of evil (1 Timothy 6:12)

schools.summary: That no city made peace with Israel, save Gibeon: 'there was not a city that made peace with the children of Israel, save the Hivites the inhabitants of Gibeon: all other they took in battle' — none made peace but Gibeon; all the rest were taken in war (the there was not a city that made peace with the children of Israel, save the Hivites the inhabitants of Gibeon, none made peace but Gibeon, the all other they took in battle, the rest taken in war, the figure, Gibeon alone sought peace, the rest fought and fell, the truth that the nations, but Gibeon, hardened against Israel and were destroyed, the figure of the few who seek peace with God and the many who resist, 'agree with thine adversary quickly,' Matthew 5:25, Gibeon the exception, the rest doomed, the peace of the few, the war of the many, vv.20); Gibeon alone making peace (the exception, Matthew 5:25) — the exception (save Gibeon); 'all other they took in battle' (the rest taken in war, the doomed nations, the figure, the few who seek peace and the many who resist, the providence, the war, the reason God hardened their hearts to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: There was not a city that made peace with the children of Israel, save the Hivites the inhabitants of Gibeon: all other they took in battle
that no city made peace with Israel, save Gibeon
Gibeon alone seeking peace, while all the rest hardened against Israel and were taken in war
the few who seek peace and the many who resist (Matthew 5:25)

schools.summary: That it was of the LORD to harden the hearts of the nations, that they should come against Israel and be destroyed: 'for it was of the LORD to harden their hearts, that they should come against Israel in battle, that he might destroy them utterly, and that they might have no favour, but that he might destroy them, as the LORD commanded Moses' — the judicial hardening — 'for it was of the LORD to harden their hearts, that they should come against Israel in battle, that he might destroy them utterly, and that they might have no favour, but that he might destroy them, as the LORD commanded Moses': God so ordered it that the nations were hardened to fight rather than seek peace, that they might fall under his righteous judgment (the witnesses noting the significance — the sovereignty of God over the hearts of men, hardening the impenitent in his just judgment, as he hardened Pharaoh, that the nations, ripe for destruction, should come to battle and perish, the hardening not of innocence but of nations long given to wickedness, 'the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full,' Genesis 15:16, now full and ripe for judgment, the lesson — that God in his sovereignty gives the impenitent up to hardness, that they fill up the measure of their sin and fall under his righteous wrath, the hardening a judicial act on those already guilty, the nations hardened as Pharaoh was, 'whom he will he hardeneth,' Romans 9:18, the truth that God's hardening is just, the vessels of wrath fitted for destruction, the judgment righteous, the figure — the righteous judgment of God on those who resist him, the impenitent given over, 'God gave them up... to a reprobate mind,' Romans 1:28, the truth that the destruction of the Canaanites was God's just judgment on a people ripe in sin, the hardening serving his righteous ends, the figure of the divine sovereignty that overrules even the rebellion of the wicked to their own just ruin, the call to fear the God who hardens the impenitent, the warning against resisting God, lest the heart be given up to hardness, the figure of the sovereignty of God in judicial hardening and the righteous destruction of the impenitent); God ordering it that the nations were hardened to fight rather than seek peace, the hardening of nations ripe in sin, as Pharaoh was hardened (the judicial hardening, Genesis 15:16; Romans 9:18) — the hardening (of the LORD); 'that he might destroy them utterly, and that they might have no favour, but that he might destroy them, as the LORD commanded Moses' (the hardening served God's just judgment, the nations ripe for destruction given over to ruin, the judgment righteous as God commanded Moses, the impenitent given over, Romans 1:28) — the figure (the divine sovereignty that overrules the rebellion of the wicked to their just ruin, the providence, the hardening, the conquest of the Anakims to follow, vv.21-22) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: How is God's hardening of the nations' hearts to be understood?

sides.position: As the sovereignty of God in judicial hardening and the righteous destruction of the impenitent - God hardening the impenitent in his just judgment as he hardened Pharaoh, the nations ripe in sin (the iniquity of the Amorites now full, Genesis 15:16) given over to come to battle and perish, God hardening whom he will (Romans 9:18) and giving the impenitent over to ruin (Romans 1:28), the destruction his righteous judgment

themes: For it was of the LORD to harden their hearts, that they should come against Israel in battle, that he might destroy them utterly, and that they might have no favour, but that he might destroy them, as the LORD commanded Moses
that it was of the LORD to harden the hearts of the nations, that they should come against Israel and be destroyed (the crux of the sovereignty of God in judicial hardening)
the sovereignty of God over the hearts of men, hardening the impenitent in his just judgment as he hardened Pharaoh, the nations ripe in sin given over to come to battle and perish
the iniquity of the Amorites now full, God hardening whom he will and giving the impenitent over, the destruction righteous (Genesis 15:16; Romans 9:18; Romans 1:28)

schools.summary: Joshua cutting off the Anakims from the mountains: 'and at that time came Joshua, and cut off the Anakims from the mountains, from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab, and from all the mountains of Judah, and from all the mountains of Israel: Joshua destroyed them utterly with their cities' — Joshua cut off the Anakims, the giants who had once terrified the spies, from the mountains (the and at that time came Joshua, and cut off the Anakims from the mountains, from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab, the Anakims cut off, the and from all the mountains of Judah, and from all the mountains of Israel: Joshua destroyed them utterly with their cities, utterly destroyed, the figure, the giants, who had once made Israel quail, now destroyed, the fear conquered, the truth that the Anakims, the dread of the spies, were cut off by Joshua, the figure of the great terrors of faith overcome by God's power, the giants fallen, 'there we saw the giants... we were in our own sight as grasshoppers,' Numbers 13:33, now destroyed, the giants conquered, the fear overcome, the victory of faith over its terrors, vv.20); Joshua cutting off the Anakims, the dread of the spies (the giants, Numbers 13:33) — the giants (cut off); 'Joshua destroyed them utterly with their cities' (the giants utterly destroyed, the fear conquered, the figure, the great terrors of faith overcome, the providence, the conquest, the remnant left in Gaza and Gath to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And at that time came Joshua, and cut off the Anakims from the mountains, from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab, and from all the mountains of Judah, and from all the mountains of Israel: Joshua destroyed them utterly with their cities
Joshua cutting off the Anakims from the mountains
Joshua cutting off the Anakims, the giants who had once made Israel quail, now destroyed
the great terror of faith overcome by God's power (Numbers 13:33)

schools.summary: That none of the Anakims remained, save in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod: 'there was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel: only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained' — the Anakims were cut off from Israel's land, a remnant only in the Philistine cities (the there was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel, none left in Israel's land, the only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained, a remnant in Philistia, the figure, the giants cleared from the land, a remnant left in the Philistine cities, the truth that the Anakims were destroyed in Israel's land, but some remained among the Philistines, whence Goliath came, 1 Samuel 17:4, the figure of the foe cleared from the inheritance but a remnant left for later trial, the giants gone from the land, a remnant in Philistia, the conquest of the giants, vv.21); the Anakims cleared from Israel's land (the conquest) — the clearing (none left); 'only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained' (a remnant in the Philistine cities, whence Goliath came, the later trial, 1 Samuel 17:4, the figure, the foe cleared but a remnant left for later trial, the providence, the remnant, the summary that Joshua took the whole land to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: There was none of the Anakims left in the land of the children of Israel: only in Gaza, in Gath, and in Ashdod, there remained
that none of the Anakims remained, save in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod
the Anakims cut off from Israel's land, a remnant only in the Philistine cities, whence Goliath would later come
the foe cleared but a remnant left for later trial (1 Samuel 17:4)

schools.summary: That Joshua took the whole land and gave it for an inheritance, and the land rested from war: 'so Joshua took the whole land, according to all that the LORD said unto Moses; and Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land rested from war' — the rest in the inheritance — 'so Joshua took the whole land, according to all that the LORD said unto Moses; and Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land rested from war': Joshua took the whole land as God had promised, gave it to Israel for an inheritance, and the land had rest from war (the witnesses noting the significance — the fulfilment of God's promise, the land possessed and divided, and the rest of the people in their inheritance after the long warfare, the whole land taken as the LORD said, given to the tribes, the war ended and the people at rest, the lesson — that God fulfils all his promises, giving his people their inheritance and rest, 'there failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken... all came to pass,' Joshua 21:45, the truth that the rest of Canaan was a figure and pledge of the greater rest of God's people, 'there remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God,' Hebrews 4:9, the figure — the rest in the inheritance fulfilled in the rest that Christ gives and the eternal rest that remains, 'come unto me... and I will give you rest,' Matthew 11:28, the truth that after the warfare of faith comes the rest of God, the inheritance possessed, the figure of the saints' rest in the heavenly Canaan, the war over and the inheritance entered, the call to press through the warfare to the promised rest, the assurance that God brings his people into their inheritance and gives them rest, the figure of the rest of God's people in the promised inheritance, a figure of the rest that remains); Joshua taking the whole land as God promised and giving it to the tribes for an inheritance, the promise fulfilled (the inheritance possessed, Joshua 21:45) — the inheritance (given to the tribes); 'And the land rested from war' (the land at rest from war after the long conquest, the rest of God's people in their inheritance, a figure of the rest that remains for the people of God, fulfilled in Christ's rest, the rest that remains, Hebrews 4:9; Matthew 11:28) — the figure (the saints' rest in the heavenly Canaan, the providence, the rest, the list of the conquered kings to follow, ch.12) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'the land rested from war' signify?

sides.position: The rest of God's people in the promised inheritance, a figure of the rest that remains - the land possessed and divided and the people at rest after the long warfare, God failing not in any good thing he had spoken (Joshua 21:45), the rest of Canaan a figure and pledge of the greater rest that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9), fulfilled in the rest that Christ gives (Matthew 11:28)

themes: So Joshua took the whole land, according to all that the LORD said unto Moses; and Joshua gave it for an inheritance unto Israel according to their divisions by their tribes. And the land rested from war
that Joshua took the whole land and gave it for an inheritance, and the land rested from war (the crux of the rest of God's people in the promised inheritance)
the fulfilment of God's promise, the land possessed and divided, and the rest of the people in their inheritance after the long warfare
a figure and pledge of the greater rest that remains for the people of God, fulfilled in the rest that Christ gives (Joshua 21:45; Hebrews 4:9; Matthew 11:28)
